results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is a pioneer video artist and computer animator who was particularly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Originally trained in architecture, he transitioned into computer graphics and electronic music around 1966, citing the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group and Bell Laboratories as early inspirations. In the late 1960s, he worked as a computer animator for the Computer Image Corporation in New York, where he operated the Scanimate system—an analog computer used for video animation. During this period, he collaborated on significant early video works, such as Ed Emshwiller's 'Scapemates' (1972). In 1972, Wright became the first artist-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center (ETC) in Binghamton, New York, where he worked extensively with the Paik-Abe Video Synthesizer and conducted workshops throughout the state. He was also an early associate of The Kitchen, the influential media arts center in New York City. Throughout the 1970s, he participated in early computer art festivals and collaborated with artists like Dimitri Devyatkin and musicians such as Sun Ra. In the 1990s, he developed the 'Video Shredder,' a real-time video performance system, and continued to perform as a 'video instrument' player in the Boston area.

group and Bell Laboratories as early inspirations.
In the late 1960s, he worked as a computer animator for the Computer Image Corporation in New York, where he operated the Scanimate system—an analog computer used for video animation.
During this period, he collaborated on significant early video works, such as Ed Emshwiller's 'Scapemates' (1972).
